subject: Biology
unit: Unit Five: The human body systems

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: What type of synovial joint allows movement in only one plane, similar to the motion of a door hinge?
questions.hint: Think of a door opening and closing along a single axis.
questions.explanation: Hinge joints permit flexion and extension in one plane, as seen in the elbow and knee. Option A, ball and socket, allows multiaxial movement (shoulder and hip).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: Hinge joints: like a door, a joints that opens and closes in one direction along one plane only (elbow and knee joints)

explanations_tiered.definition: A hinge joint is a synovial joint that permits angular movement in a single plane, typically flexion and extension.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The articular surfaces are shaped like a pulley and a spool, restricting movement to one axis.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a door hinge, the elbow only bends straight back and forth, not side to side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Hinge joints have strong collateral ligaments that prevent side-to-side motion, allowing only sagittal plane movement.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The trochlea of the humerus articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna, creating a uniaxial hinge with approximately 140° of flexion-extension range.
reasoning_steps: Recall that hinge joints move like a door.
Identify that only one plane of motion is allowed.
Examples: elbow, knee, interphalangeal joints.
misconceptions: Some think the knee is a ball-and-socket because it rotates slightly, but its primary motion is hinge-like (with some rotation).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Hinge joint: uniaxial, flexion/extension only. Examples: elbow, knee.

glossary_entry.definition: A synovial joint that allows movement in one plane, like a door hinge.

flashcard.front: What type of joint moves only in one plane (like a door)?
flashcard.back: Hinge joint — example: elbow and knee.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which joint type is uniaxial and works like a door hinge?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Hinge joint — allows flexion/extension only (elbow, knee).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Uniaxial synovial joint permitting movement in one plane only.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Hinge: one plane, like a door. Elbow, knee.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with elbow: only bends, does not rotate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Hinge joints are uniaxial; ball-and-socket are multiaxial; pivot allows rotation; saddle allows biaxial movement.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: Which type of joint allows the greatest range of motion, including flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, and rotation?
questions.hint: Consider which joint resembles a round ball fitting into a cup, allowing movement in all directions.
questions.explanation: Ball and socket joints (shoulder and hip) permit multiaxial movement: flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and rotation. Option B, condyloid joints, allow biaxial movement but not full rotation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: the rounded head of one bone sits in the cup of another bone to permit movement in all directions (shoulder and hip joints)

explanations_tiered.definition: A ball and socket joint is a multiaxial synovial joint where the spherical head of one bone fits into the cup-like cavity of another.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This design allows movement in three planes: sagittal, frontal, and transverse, providing the highest mobility.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a joystick in a rounded base — you can push it forward/back, side to side, and twist it around.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The shoulder (glenohumeral) joint has shallow socket for mobility; the hip (acetabulofemoral) has deeper socket for stability.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The articular capsule and surrounding ligaments (e.g., rotator cuff) provide stability while allowing up to 120° of abduction and 180° of flexion.
reasoning_steps: Recall that multiaxial movement requires a ball-and-socket design.
Identify that this joint allows all three planes: sagittal, frontal, transverse.
Examples: shoulder and hip.
misconceptions: Some think the hip has less mobility than shoulder, but both are ball-and-socket; hip's depth limits extreme ranges for stability.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Ball and socket: multiaxial (all directions). Shoulder, hip.

glossary_entry.definition: A multiaxial synovial joint allowing movement in all planes, including rotation.

flashcard.front: Which joint type allows the greatest range of motion (multiaxial)?
flashcard.back: Ball and socket joint — example: shoulder and hip.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of joint is the shoulder?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Ball and socket joint — multiaxial, allows movement in all planes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Multiaxial joint allowing movement in all directions; e.g., shoulder and hip.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare shoulder (mobility) vs hip (stability) as examples of the same joint type.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Ball and socket is the only joint type that allows full rotation plus all other movements.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: In which specific region of the female reproductive tract does fertilization of the ovum by a sperm cell normally occur?
questions.hint: Consider where the egg meets sperm while traveling toward the uterus.
questions.explanation: Fertilization typically occurs in the ampulla, the widest part of the fallopian tube. Option A, the uterus, is where implantation occurs, not fertilization.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: union of human egg and sperm cells in the ampulla of the fallopian tube to produce a zygote

explanations_tiered.definition: Fertilization is the fusion of a sperm cell and an ovum in the ampulla of the fallopian tube, forming a zygote.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The ampulla provides a suitable environment for sperm capacitation and egg-sperm recognition.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of the fallopian tube as a tunnel where the egg and sperm finally meet, usually in the widest part called the ampulla.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The ampulla's secretions promote sperm motility and acrosome reaction; the ovum is typically fertilized within 12–24 hours of ovulation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The ampulla's ciliated epithelium and smooth muscle create currents that guide both gametes; zona pellucida binding triggers the acrosome reaction.
reasoning_steps: Recall that fertilization occurs before the egg reaches the uterus.
Identify the fallopian tube as the site.
Specify the ampulla as the exact region.
misconceptions: Many believe fertilization occurs in the uterus; actually implantation occurs there, but fertilization is in the fallopian tube.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Fertilization site: ampulla of fallopian tube.

glossary_entry.definition: The fusion of sperm and egg in the ampulla of the fallopian tube to form a zygote.

flashcard.front: Where does fertilization normally occur in humans?
flashcard.back: In the ampulla (widest part) of the fallopian tube.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the normal site of fertilization?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Ampulla of the fallopian tube (uterine tube).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sperm-egg fusion in the fallopian tube ampulla forming a zygote.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the egg survives only ~24h; sperm survive up to 5 days.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the structure and function of the female reproductive system
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Fertilization is in the fallopian tube ampulla; implantation is in the uterus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: Which contraceptive method provides the additional benefit of reducing the risk of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) by creating a physical barrier?
questions.hint: Look for the method that physically blocks both sperm and pathogen transmission.
questions.explanation: Male condoms are barrier contraceptives that prevent pregnancy by blocking sperm and reduce STI transmission by limiting contact with bodily fluids. Option A, oral pills, alter hormones but do not block STIs.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: Condoms (male and female) are the only contraceptive methods that helps prevent sexually transmitted infections (STIs)

explanations_tiered.definition: A barrier contraceptive physically prevents sperm from reaching the egg and reduces exposure to pathogens in bodily fluids.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Latex or polyurethane condoms block the passage of sperm and also provide a mechanical barrier against viruses and bacteria.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like wearing a glove to keep your hands clean, a condom covers the penis to stop sperm and germs from getting through.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Condoms prevent direct contact between semen and the vaginal/cervical mucosa, reducing transmission of HIV, chlamydia, gonorrhea, and others.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Laboratory studies show latex condoms are impermeable to particles the size of HIV (100 nm) and hepatitis B virus (42 nm) when used correctly.
reasoning_steps: Identify that barrier methods physically block.
Recall that only condoms offer STI protection.
Eliminate hormonal methods (pills, implant) and IUD.
misconceptions: Some think all contraceptives protect against STIs; only barrier methods (especially condoms) provide that protection.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Only condoms (male/female) prevent both pregnancy and STIs.

glossary_entry.definition: A barrier contraceptive worn on the penis that prevents pregnancy and reduces STI transmission.

flashcard.front: Which contraceptive also helps prevent STIs?
flashcard.back: Male and female condoms — they create a physical barrier.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why are condoms unique among contraceptives?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They also reduce STI transmission by forming a physical barrier.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Contraceptive that physically blocks sperm from reaching the egg; condoms also block pathogens.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Condoms: pregnancy + STI protection. Hormonal methods: no STI protection.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Stress that condoms are the only contraceptive that protects against HIV.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain ways to prevent or reduce the spread of STIs/HIV
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Only condoms provide dual protection (pregnancy and STI); hormonal methods and IUDs do not block pathogens.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: The joint between the atlas (C1) and the dens of the axis (C2) that allows the head to rotate "no" is an example of which type of joint?
questions.hint: Think of a pivot like a door swiveling on a hinge pin.
questions.explanation: The atlantoaxial joint is a pivot joint where the dens of the axis acts as a pivot around which the atlas rotates. Option A, hinge, allows only flexion/extension, not rotation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: rotary or trochoid joints in which one bone swivels in a ring (ulna, radius, and neck)

explanations_tiered.definition: A pivot joint is a uniaxial synovial joint where a rounded bone rotates within a ring formed by another bone and ligament.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The dens of the axis fits within the anterior arch of the atlas and is held by the transverse ligament, allowing rotation of the head.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a spinning stool: the seat (atlas) rotates around the central pole (dens of axis) to turn your head side to side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The median atlantoaxial joint is a true pivot joint; the odontoid process serves as the pivot point, permitting approximately 50° of rotation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The transverse ligament binds the dens to the atlas; alar ligaments limit excessive rotation. Damage to these ligaments (e.g., in rheumatoid arthritis) can cause atlantoaxial instability.
reasoning_steps: Identify the motion: rotation around a vertical axis.
Recall that pivot joints are designed for rotation.
Match to example: atlas rotating around axis dens.
misconceptions: Some confuse pivot with ball-and-socket; pivot is uniaxial (rotation only), ball-and-socket is multiaxial.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Pivot joint: uniaxial rotation. Example: atlas/axis (head 'no').

glossary_entry.definition: A uniaxial joint where one bone rotates within a ring formed by another bone and ligament.

flashcard.front: What joint type allows the head to rotate 'no'?
flashcard.back: Pivot joint — atlantoaxial joint (atlas rotating around dens of axis).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of joint is the atlantoaxial joint?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Pivot joint — allows rotation of the head ("no" motion).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Uniaxial joint permitting only rotation; e.g., atlantoaxial joint.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate by having students rotate head side to side; explain the dens as pivot.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The atlantoaxial joint is a classic pivot joint, not a hinge (which would only allow nodding 'yes').

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: What is the average duration of human gestation calculated from the first day of the last menstrual period (LMP)?
questions.hint: Standard clinical calculation counts from LMP to delivery.
questions.explanation: Human gestation averages 40 weeks (280 days) from the last menstrual period, which includes about two weeks before ovulation. Option A (38 weeks) is the approximate post-conception duration, not LMP-based.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: Pregnancy lasts about 40 weeks (280 days) to delivery counted from the last menstrual period

explanations_tiered.definition: Gestation is the period of fetal development inside the uterus, typically lasting 40 weeks from the last menstrual period.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Clinical dating uses LMP because ovulation and conception dates are often unknown; post-conception duration is about 38 weeks.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Pregnancy usually lasts about nine calendar months, or 40 weeks from the first day of the last period.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The 40-week count includes the two weeks before ovulation; the embryo develops for approximately 266 days (38 weeks) after fertilization.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Gestational age is calculated using Naegele's rule: LMP + 280 days. Variation of ±1 week is normal, and post-term pregnancy is >42 weeks.
reasoning_steps: Recall that pregnancy is dated from LMP.
Standard duration is 40 weeks (280 days).
Differentiate from fertilization age (38 weeks).
misconceptions: Many think pregnancy is exactly 9 months; 9 calendar months ≈ 39 weeks, close to 40 weeks LMP-based.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Human gestation: 40 weeks from LMP (38 weeks post-fertilization).

glossary_entry.definition: The period of fetal development in the uterus, averaging 40 weeks from the last menstrual period.

flashcard.front: How long is human pregnancy from the last menstrual period?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the average length of human pregnancy from LMP?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Period of fetal development; average 40 weeks from last menstrual period.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish between gestational age (LMP) and fertilization age (post-conception).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain family planning strategies and pregnancy
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Standard obstetric dating uses 40 weeks from LMP; 38 weeks is the post-conception duration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: A diaphragm is a barrier contraceptive that works by physically covering which structure to prevent sperm from entering the uterus?
questions.hint: Consider which structure acts as the gateway between the vagina and the uterus.
questions.explanation: The diaphragm is a dome-shaped cup inserted into the vagina to cover the cervix, blocking sperm from passing into the uterus. Option A is incorrect because the diaphragm sits deeper, covering the cervix specifically.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: Diaphragm is a small and soft silicon dome placed inside the vagina to stop sperm from entering the uterus

explanations_tiered.definition: A diaphragm is a reusable silicone barrier that covers the cervix to prevent sperm from entering the uterus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It is used with spermicide and must be inserted before intercourse and left in place for several hours afterward.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a shower cap that fits tightly over the cervix, blocking the doorway to the uterus.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The diaphragm's rim fits behind the pubic bone and sits over the cervical os, creating a mechanical barrier that must be correctly positioned.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Failure rates (3–14%) are partly due to improper fitting or displacement; spermicide increases efficacy by immobilizing sperm that might bypass the barrier.
reasoning_steps: Recall that the diaphragm is a barrier placed in the vagina.
Identify that it specifically covers the cervix.
Understand that blocking the cervix prevents sperm from reaching the uterus.
misconceptions: Some think the diaphragm covers the entire vagina; it only covers the cervix, and the vaginal walls must hold it in place.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Diaphragm: covers cervix → blocks sperm from uterus.

glossary_entry.definition: A silicone barrier device placed over the cervix to prevent sperm from entering the uterus.

flashcard.front: What structure does a contraceptive diaphragm cover?
flashcard.back: The cervix — to block sperm from entering the uterus.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does a diaphragm prevent pregnancy?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It covers the cervix, physically blocking sperm from entering the uterus.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A barrier contraceptive that covers the cervix to prevent sperm passage.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that fitting by a healthcare provider is necessary for effectiveness.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The diaphragm specifically covers the cervix; the vaginal opening is covered by female condoms, not diaphragms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: How do combined oral contraceptive pills (containing estrogen and progestin) primarily prevent pregnancy?

options.A: They thicken cervical mucus and inhibit ovulation
options.B: They permanently block the fallopian tubes
options.C: They create a physical barrier in the vagina
options.D: They kill sperm immediately after ejaculation

questions.hint: Think about hormonal effects on the ovary and cervical secretions.
questions.explanation: Combined oral contraceptives suppress ovulation by inhibiting FSH/LH and thicken cervical mucus to impede sperm passage. Option B describes tubal ligation (sterilization), not a hormonal pill.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: prevents the ovaries from releasing an egg each month (ovulation) ... thickens the mucus in the neck of the womb

explanations_tiered.definition: Combined oral contraceptives use synthetic estrogen and progestin to suppress ovulation and alter cervical mucus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: They provide negative feedback to the hypothalamus and pituitary, reducing GnRH, FSH, and LH, thereby preventing follicular development.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pill sends a 'stop' signal to the ovaries so no egg is released, and makes the mucus too thick for sperm to swim through.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Progestin thickens cervical mucus (creating a sperm barrier) and thins the endometrium, while both hormones suppress the LH surge required for ovulation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Continuous exogenous progestin inhibits the mid-cycle LH surge, prevents ovulation, and also reduces tubal motility and alters endometrial receptivity (post-fertilization effect).
reasoning_steps: Identify that oral pills are hormonal, not barrier.
Recall that hormones suppress ovulation (primary mechanism).
Recognize secondary mechanism: thickened cervical mucus.
misconceptions: Some think the pill causes abortion; it primarily prevents ovulation and fertilization, though it may also affect implantation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Combined pill: inhibits ovulation + thickens cervical mucus.

glossary_entry.definition: A birth control pill containing estrogen and progestin that prevents ovulation and thickens cervical mucus.

flashcard.front: How do combined birth control pills work?
flashcard.back: They inhibit ovulation and thicken cervical mucus, preventing sperm passage.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What are the two main mechanisms of combined oral contraceptives?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Hormonal pill containing estrogen and progestin that prevents ovulation and thickens cervical mucus.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with progestin-only pill (POP) which primarily thickens mucus and may not always inhibit ovulation.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Hormonal pills prevent ovulation and thicken mucus; barrier methods physically block, not hormonal.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: Which type of joint allows only limited sliding or gliding movements between flat or slightly curved bone surfaces?
questions.hint: Think of the small bones in the wrist that slide over each other.
questions.explanation: Gliding (plane) joints allow limited non-axial movement as flat surfaces slip past one another, as in intercarpal joints. Option A (hinge) allows angular movement in one plane, not sliding.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: a plane joint that allows only limited movement by slipping smooth surfaces over one another (wrist joint)

explanations_tiered.definition: A gliding joint (plane joint) is a synovial joint with flat or slightly curved articular surfaces that allow sliding movements.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: These joints are non-axial or have very limited multi-directional movement, providing stability rather than range.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine stacking two smooth Lego bricks; you can slide them a little side to side or front to back, but not rotate much.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Gliding joints occur between carpal bones, tarsal bones, and the acromioclavicular joint; movement is limited by tight capsules and ligaments.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The intercarpal joints allow approximately 0.5–2 mm of translation; they function as part of a coordinated system to enable combined wrist motions like circumduction.
reasoning_steps: Recall joint names and their primary motions.
Gliding = sliding flat surfaces.
Example: wrist carpals, acromioclavicular joint.
misconceptions: Some think the wrist is a condyloid joint (radiocarpal is condyloid), but intercarpal joints are gliding joints.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Gliding joint: limited sliding (e.g., wrist bones).

glossary_entry.definition: A synovial joint where flat bone surfaces slide over each other, allowing limited non-axial movement.

flashcard.front: What type of joint allows only sliding movements?
flashcard.back: Gliding (plane) joint — example: between carpal bones.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What movement do gliding joints permit?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Limited sliding or gliding in multiple directions but with small amplitude.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Synovial joint allowing limited sliding movement; e.g., between carpal bones.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with saddle joint (thumb) which allows biaxial movement but no rotation.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the structure and function of the musculoskeletal system
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Gliding joints are the only type where the primary motion is sliding, not angular or rotational.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
questions.question: The copper-bearing intrauterine device (IUD) primarily prevents pregnancy by creating an inflammatory response that is toxic to sperm and also by:

options.B: Thickening cervical mucus and thinning the uterine lining
options.D: Killing sperm with a spermicidal coating

questions.hint: Consider how the IUD affects both the cervical environment and the endometrium.
questions.explanation: Copper IUDs release copper ions that are toxic to sperm and also thicken cervical mucus and thin the endometrium, preventing implantation. Option A describes hormonal IUDs, not copper IUDs.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit Five: The human body systems
source_trace.content_anchor: thickens the cervical mucus and thins the uterine lining

explanations_tiered.definition: A copper IUD is a small T-shaped device inserted into the uterus that releases copper ions, creating a spermicidal environment.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Copper ions impair sperm motility and viability, and the inflammatory response alters the endometrium to prevent implantation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The copper IUD works like a copper 'shield' in the uterus that stops sperm from swimming and makes the womb lining unwelcoming.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Copper ions generate reactive oxygen species that damage sperm DNA and membranes; the local inflammation also thickens cervical mucus.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Copper IUDs have a failure rate of <1% and provide up to 10 years of continuous contraception without systemic hormones, as copper is not absorbed.
reasoning_steps: Identify that copper IUD is non-hormonal.
Recall it releases copper ions toxic to sperm.
Secondary effect: thickened mucus + thinned endometrium.
misconceptions: Some think copper IUD works only by preventing fertilization; it also has post-fertilization effects (prevents implantation).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Copper IUD: toxic to sperm + thickens mucus + thins endometrium.

glossary_entry.definition: A non-hormonal intrauterine device that releases copper ions, toxic to sperm, and alters the uterine lining.

flashcard.front: How does a copper IUD prevent pregnancy?
flashcard.back: Copper ions kill sperm, thicken cervical mucus, and thin the endometrium.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What makes the copper IUD effective without hormones?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Copper ions are spermicidal; also thickens mucus and thins endometrium.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Non-hormonal intrauterine device using copper ions for contraception.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Copper IUD: spermicidal + mucus thickening + endometrial thinning.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Distinguish copper IUD (non-hormonal, long-acting) from hormonal IUD (e.g., Mirena).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Copper IUDs act locally via copper ion toxicity, mucus thickening, and endometrial thinning; hormonal IUDs release progestin to inhibit ovulation.
